8:
"And it came to pass in the morning that his spirit was troubled; and he sent and called for all the magicians of Egypt, and all the wise men thereof: and Pharaoh told them his dream; but there was none that could interpret them unto Pharaoh." The witnesses note Pharaoh's troubled spirit and the failure of all Egypt's wisdom before the God-sent dream. Gill notes his spirit was troubled "with the thoughts of his dreams; they were uppermost in his mind; he was continually thinking of them," as if he had always the same images before him awake as asleep, and therefore could not be easy without getting knowledge of their meaning. Wesley observes that "it cannot but put us into a concern to receive any extraordinary message from heaven."

The magicians and wise men the commentators describe from Egypt's own antiquity. Keil and Delitzsch derive the word for magicians from the stylus: the sacred scribes of the priestly caste, "who occupied themselves with the sacred arts and sciences of the Egyptians, the hieroglyphic writings, astrology, the interpretation of dreams, the foretelling of events, magic, and conjuring," and were regarded as possessors of secret arts (Exodus 7:11). Clarke likewise makes them Josephus's "sacred scribes, or professors of sacred learning," and the wise men those who, as Porphyry describes, passed their whole life in the contemplation of divine things, in self-purification, arithmetic, geometry, and hymns — the sages among whom Pythagoras conversed. Ellicott adds that their real learning carried with it a claim to "an occult and mysterious acquaintance with the secrets of the gods," and supposes that "probably many of the wise men made the attempt, but in such an imperfect manner as not to be able to satisfy Pharaoh's mind."

The sharper wonder, Keil and Delitzsch observe, is that the clue lay in Egypt's own religious symbols — the cow was the emblem of Isis, goddess of the all-sustaining earth, standing in the hieroglyphics for the earth, agriculture, and food, and the overflowing Nile was the source of the land's fertility — yet not one of them could read the dream. Calvin finds the whole scene ordered from above. The king's anxiety "was as an inward seal of the Spirit of God, to give authenticity to the dream"; yet Pharaoh, convinced the dream portended something great, "seeks out imposters, who would darken, by their fallacies, the light which was divinely kindled." That these boastful diviners gave the credulous king no answer at all Calvin can only ascribe to God: "the Lord so strikes dumb the wicked workers of deceit, that they cannot even find a specious explanation of the dreams"; and by their silence the king's anxiety is sharpened, "because he considers that what has escaped the sagacity of the magicians must be something very serious and secret." We are taught, he adds, to count it "a singular favor, when either false prophets are silenced, or their fatuity is, in any manner, discovered to us."

So the witnesses make the verse the failure of Egypt's boasted wisdom — the whole company of her diviners, soothsayers, and sages utterly at a loss before the things of God. Wesley draws the design plainly: the magicians "were puzzled; the rules of their art failed them; these dreams of Pharaoh did not fall within the compass of them," and this "was to make Joseph's performance by the Spirit of God the more admirable" — God letting all the wise men of that learned land fall dumb, that the glory of the interpretation might be his alone, through his servant Joseph.

On "the famine waxed sore in the land of Egypt," the grammatical-historical note the severity: "the famine waxed sore in the land of Egypt" — the famine grew severe (the dearth pressing); the need for the stored corn; the providence using Joseph's provision to sustain Egypt (and the nations, v.57); the saving plan in action. The verse displays Joseph opening the storehouses in the famine.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the famine — "the famine was over all the face of the earth: And Joseph opened all the storehouses, and sold unto the Egyptians," the dispensing (vv.48-49, Gill, Clarke); "opened all the storehouses, and sold unto the Egyptians," the provision (Clarke, Gill); "the famine waxed sore," the severity (v.57, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dispensing Joseph. The verse marks the famine: over the earth. The verse displays the storehouses: opened. The verse displays the corn: sold. The verse displays the severity: sore. The dispensing displays Joseph supplying bread in the famine. The verse displays the Joseph: opening the stores. The verse displays the Egyptians: supplied. The verse displays the famine: severe. Joseph opening the storehouses in the famine displays the saving provision: Joseph opened the storehouses and sold to the Egyptians as the famine waxed sore (vv.48-49) — the rejected son dispensing the bread that saved the land. The verse displays the dispensing Joseph, sought by all nations (v.57).
57:
"And all countries came into Egypt to Joseph for to buy corn; because that the famine was so sore in all lands." The witnesses note all nations coming to Joseph for corn. On "all countries came into Egypt to Joseph for to buy corn," the grammatical voices note the nations. JFB and Gill note that "all countries came into Egypt to Joseph for to buy corn" — the surrounding nations (Canaan, and others, the famine being widespread, v.54) came to Egypt, to Joseph, to buy corn; the rejected son the provider for the world; the nations seeking bread from him; the providence drawing all to Joseph (and so bringing Jacob's house, 42:1-2).

On "came into Egypt to Joseph," the reformed and witnesses note the dispenser. Gill notes that all came "to Joseph" — the one dispenser of the bread; the rejected son to whom all the world resorted for life; the type (the fathers) of Christ, to whom all nations are gathered for the bread of life (John 6:35; Isaiah 45:22, "look unto me, and be ye saved, all the ends of the earth").

On "because that the famine was so sore in all lands," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the extent: "because that the famine was so sore in all lands" — the famine severe everywhere (v.54); the nations driven to Egypt for bread; the providence using the famine to bring all to Joseph (and Jacob's sons, ch. 42, the way to the reunion and the saving of the house, 45:5-7); the rejected son the saviour of many peoples. The verse displays all nations coming to Joseph for corn.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the nations — "all countries came into Egypt to Joseph for to buy corn," the world resorting to Joseph (42:1-2, JFB, Gill); "came into Egypt to Joseph," the dispenser of bread (John 6:35, Gill); "because that the famine was so sore in all lands," the widespread famine (v.54; 45:5-7, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the resorting nations. The verse marks the countries: all. The verse displays the corn: to buy. The verse displays the Joseph: the dispenser. The verse displays the type: Christ. The resort displays all the world coming to Joseph for bread. The verse displays the nations: coming. The verse displays the Joseph: the provider. The verse displays the providence: gathering. All nations coming to Joseph for corn display the rejected son the saviour of the world: all countries came to Joseph to buy corn, the famine being sore in all lands (42:1-2; John 6:35) — the world resorting to him for bread, a type of Christ to whom all nations are gathered for life, the providence bringing even Jacob's house (45:5-7). The verse closes the chapter, before the brothers come (ch.42).
